summary:
The Roman persecution of Christians (c. 64–313 AD) was a sporadic but significant series of state actions against the growing Christian movement — ranging from local mob violence to systematic empire-wide persecutions — that shaped early Christian identity, theology, and community organization profoundly. The first major persecution came under Nero in 64 AD, who blamed Christians for the [[event:2017|Great Fire of Rome]] and executed many in theatrical displays in the arena. Paul the Apostle and Peter were traditionally martyred in Rome during this period. However, for most of the 1st–2nd centuries, persecution was local and inconsistent — there was no empire-wide anti-Christian law, and the threat varied enormously by province and governor.

Persecution intensified significantly in the 3rd century during periods of imperial crisis, when emperors sought to unify the empire through religious conformity. Decius (249–251 AD) issued the first empire-wide decree requiring all subjects to sacrifice to Roman gods and obtain a certificate of compliance, triggering the first systematic empire-wide persecution. Valerian targeted Christian clergy specifically. The [[event:2822|Great Persecution]] under Diocletian (303–311 AD) was the most severe — ordering destruction of churches, burning of scriptures, and execution of clergy. Paradoxically, the visible courage of Christian martyrs (whose accounts were preserved and spread as martyrologies) strengthened rather than destroyed Christianity's appeal, demonstrating to observers the depth of Christian commitment. The persecutions ended definitively with [[event:2028|the Edict of Milan]] in 313 AD.
